audit_status.source_base: Greek Romans text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for Abraham, counted righteousness/justice, David, circumcision sign, promise, trust apart from Law, life from dead.

verses.restored: What then shall we say Abraham, our forefather according to flesh, has found?
For if Abraham was made righteous/just from works, he has boast—but not toward the Divine.
For what does the Scripture say? Abraham trusted the Divine, and it was counted to him as righteousness/justice.
Now to the one working, the wage is not counted according to grace but according to debt.
But to the one not working yet trusting upon the One making righteous/just the ungodly, that one's trust is counted as righteousness/justice.
Just as David also speaks of the blessedness of the human to whom the Divine counts righteousness/justice apart from works:
Blessed/flourishing are those whose lawless deeds were released and whose sins/misalignments were covered;
blessed/flourishing is the man to whom the Lord will certainly not count sin/misalignment.
Is this blessedness then upon the circumcision, or also upon the uncircumcision? For we say trust was counted to Abraham as righteousness/justice.
How then was it counted? While he was in circumcision or in uncircumcision? Not in circumcision, but in uncircumcision.

And he received a sign of circumcision, a seal of the righteousness/justice of the trust he had while uncircumcised, so that he might be father of all those trusting through uncircumcision, so that righteousness/justice might be counted to them,
and father of circumcision to those not only from circumcision but also walking in the footsteps of the trust of our father Abraham while uncircumcised.
For the promise to Abraham or to his seed that he would be heir of the world was not through Law, but through righteousness/justice of trust.
For if those from Law are heirs, trust has been emptied and the promise made inactive.
For Law works wrath; but where there is no Law, neither is there transgression.

Because of this it is from trust, so that it may be according to grace, so that the promise may be firm to all the seed, not only to the one from Law but also to the one from Abraham's trust, who is father of us all,
just as it is written: I have made you father of many nations—before the Divine whom he trusted, the One making the dead alive and calling the things not being as being.
Against hope, upon hope he trusted, so that he might become father of many nations, according to what had been spoken: So shall your seed be.
Not weakening in trust, he considered his own body already deadened, being about a hundred years old, and the deadening of Sarah's womb.
He did not waver in distrust toward the promise of the Divine, but was empowered in trust, giving glory to the Divine,
being fully assured that what he had promised, he was also powerful to do.
Therefore it was also counted to him as righteousness/justice.
But it was not written because of him alone that it was counted to him,
but also because of us, to whom it is about to be counted: to those trusting upon the One who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead,
who was handed over because of our trespasses and raised because of our being made righteous/just.

translation_notes: Romans 4 is structured around Genesis 15 and Psalm 32. Abraham is made the ancestor of both circumcised and uncircumcised trust, preserving Paul's nations argument without erasing Israel's covenant story.
